proposal_text:
Proposal number: 1333885

Proposal: LET'S WALK! - climate change awareness day event

Summary


Purpose: helping to rise the climate change awareness our world so urgently needs. 

Suggested actions: During that day we encourage people to walk and to:

leave their cars at home,
skip the bus,
avoid the subway and
turn to their own bodies in order to move around the city, by walking, by using their bicycles, their rollers, their skates.
If you are not lucky enough to be living near your work space you can still collaborate by doing home office and other activities such as walking to the grocery or the club.
Using a smartphone app we intend to track how much people walk during the day of the event. This information will then be published by social media and traditional media. The data provided by the app will also be translate in the reductions already mentioned, calculating savings in relationship  with the kilometrers walked and avoiding using transportation during that day.The application may use a similar alogorithm that "calculate your ecological footprint" uses, that is available at the Government web page [14].
Because of the altruistic spirit of the cause, we expect to have support from the local ONG´s, health organizations and the government.     
Main value generation loops v1.0.png


Short term Impacts: we expect to save energy and money, GHG emissions, and we will contribute to build sustainable habits, for a healthy environment in the war against the dangers of a sedentary lifestyle.  

Long term impacts:

Since the proposal is to make people aware of how important it is to care about the planet. More climate change aware people would naturally support the following initiatives:

Recycling: We forecast that it could increase by 10 %,
 
Energy savings: recommendations use increment by 10 %
 
Distributed solar photovoltaic electricity law: is approved and covers 5 % of city demand.
 
Electric buses: their use is implemented in 2 % of city bus lines.
Is this proposal for a practice or a project?
Practice

What actions do you propose?
a) The main thrust of “Let’s Walk” is walking, because Buenos Aires has a high population density, and distances might be shorter than in other cities, so walking to work is a real possibility for some people. People can

Walk 10 - 15 blocks to their offices or back home.

Use public transportation, including buses, underground / subways and then walk the rest of the way to their offices or homes.

 

b) Cycling also adds value to the “Let’s walk” proposal for the population 10 to 30 blocks from their office. The Buenos Aires City already has implemented an “ecobici” (eco bikes) program [15]. The ecobici system has bikes parked in several distributed city points, and anyone can use one of these bikes for free using his / her document. You just return the bike in another bike parking place near your destination.

 

REVIEW OF SOME “BIKE TO WORK” PROGRAMS IN CANADA AND USA:

USA Today informs that Bike to Work programs might be rising in US [21].

Through a google search we found the following relevant links:

table-bike-to-work-projects.png


ANSWERS TO SOME OBJECTIONS TO “BIKE TO WORK”

The Huffpost article [16] mentions 9 objections to “Bike to work”, here are our thoughts about them!

main-objections.png

“LET’S WALK BUSINESS MODEL CANVAS”

LET`S WALK PROJECT BUSINESS CANVAS v2.0.png

As we know we have different partners in this event, which play a fundamental role.

On one side, the people enrolled in the event, that will participate registering using a mobile app. They will either walk to their works or commute by bike. In order to be eligible to receive a prize, each participant will allow the app to register information about distances walked / biked, which transportation they usually use to commute in order to calculate GHG emission savings due to the event. Those people that contribute with the biggest GHG emission savings will win prizes as well as  benefits from companies that sponsor the event.

It´s necessary to distinguish, that by incentivizing the movement we are preventing obesity, decrease bad cholesterol and increase good cholesterol, improving the state of mind and incorporating healthy habits, so typically HMOs and the city government will be very much interested, as in Argentina poor people use public hospitals maintained by city taxes.

On the other side, we need to be able to organize this event with which we need the Government's support to convey the event to the society. It will be benefited by an increased use of the public transport by the population, better flow of the traffic (less cars in the streets)  and at the same time it will reaffirm it´s commitment with the environment.

In addition to this, it will be a great opportunity to advertise the use of the eco-bikes(“ecobicis”) [15] and, as a result, the bike lanes.

On the other hand, the information generated by the app (big data)  will be of great utility since it will show which are the population's needs (for example: the best locations for entrances of subways , routes of buses or even which are the areas that are disconnected and need public transport).

It is also important to have the companies that will join on this great event allowing the use of “home office”. In addition, they will collaborate as sponsors and obtain in exchange Big Data to improve its business and find niche.

Sponsorships are one of the best ways of promoting a business and winning popularity between the target and generate good marketing for a brand. It can be specially used to increase credibility, image and prestige of the brand.

All participation is voluntary, and public transportation will still be available to those who want to use it.

LGC Team will ask for suggestions and advice from NGOs(such as WWF, who implemented "The earth hour"). In order to have more tools to guide the team on how to develop the project. 
 
Once LCG Team recieves feedback from NGOs.They will present the formal proposal to the Institute of Technology of Buenos Aires (ITBA) and to the MIT Colab
 
The team is looking forward to receive feedback from CLIMATE COLAB in order to improve the project. ITBA will give their consent and support to it.
 
We received the recognition and prize from MIT Colab thanks to the backing through votes from ITBA.
 
LGC Team will contact several organizations to help with fundraising and to add value to the final event (GOOGLE, MASS MEDIA, SPONSORS, etc). Nevertheless to make it happen we need firstly the GOVERNMENT authorization.
 
The government will notificate the companies of the proposal and ask for their cooperation by allowing the employees to work from home. They will also have to arrange with the transportation about the modification of their routes.
 
Distance walked and GHG savings: To have an estimation of the GHGs saved is necessary an app (develop by GOOGLE if possible) that will measure them.The application may use a similar alogorithm that "calculate your ecological footprint" uses, that is available at the Government web page.But it is important to keep record of the route like Google Maps does. 
 
Once the proposal is accepted the next step is to settle a date and communicate it to the society, a key actor in this process is MASS MEDIA. 
 
The day of the event SPONSORS will play an important role by motivating the population to walk more. This could be achived by offering refreshments and prizes. 
 
Finally,all the information is collected by GOOGLE. Including steps per person and the equivalence with the reduction of GHGs obteined.
 
The LGC Team will analyze the results and make papers to be published and to communicate it to the community, the government and the MIT. 
 
As the results will be positive this idea can be implemented in other countries, all over the world. By this the proposal will reach a greater impact and a change in people's habits worldwide.


Who will take these actions?
LGC Team at ITBA: designs proposal and presents it to Climate Colab.
 
Argentine Government sponsors "LET'S WALK day"
 
Media covers the events publishing metrics on energy saved, emissions saved and money saved.
 
5 % local city population participates in the "LET'S WALK day"
 
NGOs sponsor event.
 
Transportation works as usual.

Other countries can implement the proposal to have a higher impact
Where will these actions be taken?
City level: The first objective is to do it in Buenos Aires City as is the most populated region, the most contaminating one and the principal concentration of companies.

Country level: Later by the positive results our goal is to carry out the experience at country level, in cities like Cordoba, Rosario, Mendoza and San Luis.

Worldwide: Finally, other countries can use the proposal to make their own impacts since everything counts.

 

In addition, specify the country or countries where these actions will be taken.
Argentina

Country 2
Brazil

Country 3
Chile

Country 4
United States

Country 5
No country selected

Impact/Benefits
What impact will these actions have on greenhouse gas emissions and/or adapting to climate change?
1) City of Buenos Aires

a) Short term CO2e emissions saved in Buenos Aires city:

We postulate that 5 % of the population will participate in the event.
Public transportation use: 80% of population [1]

 
Emissions saved by cars commuting from Buenos Aires Province:

10410352 cars/month [2] /30 days/month x 15 km/trip  x 0.1 kg CO2eq / km [3] x 0.05 ( 5 % population participates  in event) = 26 tCO2e (metric tons) / day.
 

Emission saved by cars inside the city:

2.89 million people x 5% people participation x 20 % people use cars x 7 km average trip to work x 2 trips /day x 0.1 kg CO2eq / km [3] = 40.46 tCO2e / day
 

Total short term CO2e savings = (26 + 40.46) = 66.46 tCO2e /day

b) Long term CHG savings (5 years) in Buenos Aires city:

We forecast that recycling could increase by 10 %. Today only 23% of Buenos Aires city recycle [4]. And recycling saves from 0.34 to 10 Tonnes of CO2e from each ton of recycled material [5]. Considering an average 1 Tonne of CO2e saved per Tonne of recycled material, current Buenos Aires savings from recycling would be 976 Tonnes of domiciliary residues [6] x 23% people recycle x 0.1 (10 % recycling increase ) x 1 tCO2e/1 t recycled material = 22 Tonnes of CO2e / year saved.
 
Energy savings recommendations [7] use increment by 10 %. The average electricity consumption per electrified household in Argentina is 3600 kWh [11], so we estimate that the savings will be of 360 kWh/home per year * 963 thousand homes * 0.05 (5 % of participation) = 17334 kWh per year. This could save up to 17334 kWh x 0.0005925 tCO2e/kWh = 10.2 tCO2e/year
Distributed solar photo voltaic electricity law is approved and covers 1 % of city demand.
2.89 million people x 4100 kWh/person [10] x 0.01 (1% of demand) x 0.0005925 tCO2e/kWh = 70205 tCO2e/year
 
It incentivates the use of public transport in this way, so the Government must make it more efficient.For example, the use of electric buses is implemented in 2 % of city.[8] [9] Each bus will save 175 metrics tones of pollutant emissions and 22.700 liters of fuel per unit and year. We estimate that there are 10200 buses in Buenos Aires,so 2% is 204, the total reduction will be 204*175 mtones= 35700 metric tones of pollutant / year saved.[10]
 
Total emission savings = (22 + 10.2 + 70205 + 35700) = 105937 tCO2e/year

2) Extrapolation to world:

a) Short term:

66.46 tCO2e/(2.89million people) x (1087 million people) = 24997 tCO2e

b) Long term:

105.937 tCO2e/year /(2.89million people) x (1087 million people) = 39 million tCO2e / year.

The transportation sector accounts from 19 % to 25 % of total GHGs emissions, according to [12] and [13]

So the total GHG due to the transportation sector may be calculated as

46.000 million tCO2e x 22% = 10.120 million tCO2e

So the proposal savings would be 39 / 10120 = 0.38 % of the transportation sector emissions.

What are other key benefits?
Generate awareness on using more public transport
Government will have to improve the efficiency of public transport
Change the habits of the society towards a sustainable and ecological life
Smog and sound pollution will be reduced
People´s stress levels will be reduced
Increase the use of bicycles
Change habits and weaken sedentary lifestyle
Cut costs of transport and so combustibles
By implemeting home-office besides helping this cause, the employeers will have a cut down of their costs
Decrease GHGs emissions by reducing the use of cars and public transport
Costs/Challenges
What are the proposal’s projected costs?
1) Cost of security forces involved in organizing the event.

2) Cost of health support personnel, ambulances, paramedics,

3) Cost of traditional media ads

4) Cost of blog, SN ads and  social media managers / influencers

5) Cost of winner prizes

6) Cost of collaboration agreement with smartphone apps that calculate distances walked by people participating in the event

7) Cost of webpage that summarizes energy savings, money saved, GHGs emission saved.

Timeline
The day of the event, there will be an immediate impact by the reduction of the GHGs emissions.
In the short-term we are looking forward to make people realize that their can make a huge change on a daily basis.For example, they can avoid the use of cars in short distances.
Over more, we can affirm that one of important change will be the use bikes. Nowadays, Buenos Aires city provides free bikes and bicicles in the downtown. Our idea is to massify these kind of habits and became a green country free of sedentarism.
What's more, we want to Incentive companies to stablish a home-office day. This will allow people who live far from their jobs to colaborate to this cause.
Medium-term: These "Homeoffice days" could at first be once a year, but once people catch the habit, it could be done monthly.
Long-term: Eventually people could do these "Homeoffice days" weekly, so that people enjoy life a little more and savings of energy, money and GHGs emissions are multiplied.

feasibility.rationale: The event relies on a smartphone app, coordination with government, NGOs, and sponsors, and assumes 5 % city participation. While technically doable, the proposal lacks concrete budgets, risk mitigation, and a detailed implementation plan, creating moderate uncertainty about execution.

novelty.rationale: Car‑free or walk‑to‑work days have been implemented worldwide; the added elements (prize incentives, data sharing) are incremental rather than fundamentally new.

impact.rationale: Emission‑saving calculations depend on several unverified assumptions (participation rate, average trip length, emission factors). Even if the numbers were accurate, a single‑day event would contribute <0.5 % of transport sector emissions, so the long‑term systemic impact appears limited.

presentation.rationale: The document contains many typographical errors, inconsistent formatting, and duplicated sections, which hinder clear understanding despite the inclusion of some quantitative estimates.

A1.perspective_summary: From a scientific‑systems viewpoint, the proposal outlines a plausible one‑day awareness event but rests on optimistic participation and emission‑reduction assumptions. The causal chain (walking → reduced car trips → measurable GHG savings) is plausible, yet the magnitude is highly uncertain. The plan would need stronger empirical grounding and a clearer operational roadmap to be credible.
key_concerns: Assumed 5 % city participation lacks empirical justification.
Emission factors and trip‑length averages are not sourced to peer‑reviewed data.
App accuracy for distance and mode detection is not demonstrated.
Behavioral persistence after a single event is uncertain; long‑term impact may be negligible.
Cost estimates are vague; no budget or financing plan is provided.
Reliance on government and corporate cooperation is not quantified, creating implementation risk.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal depends on a suite of inter‑agency actions (government authorization, transport route adjustments, public‑private data sharing, security and health services) but provides no concrete institutional arrangements, budget line items, or legal basis for data collection. Without a clear procurement process, privacy safeguards, and a dedicated coordinating body, implementation is unlikely to succeed at the scale claimed.

novelty.rationale: A city‑wide “walk day” with a tracking app and prize incentives is a hybrid of existing car‑free day initiatives and citizen‑science apps. The combination is moderately original for Buenos Aires but not a fundamentally new policy instrument.

impact.rationale: The quantitative emissions reductions are based on optimistic participation rates and simplified calculations. Even if achieved, the absolute impact (≈66 tCO₂e per day) is modest relative to city‑wide transport emissions, though the awareness and habit‑forming potential could generate longer‑term benefits.

presentation.rationale: The document is fragmented, contains numerous typographical errors, and lacks a concise executive summary, clear timeline, and defined responsibilities. Critical governance details (e.g., who will manage the app, how data will be validated) are buried in narrative text.

A2.perspective_summary: From a governance standpoint, the proposal outlines an appealing public‑engagement event but falls short on institutional design, legal compliance, and financing. While the concept is moderately novel and could raise awareness, the lack of a concrete implementation framework makes the plan difficult to operationalize at city scale.
key_concerns: Absence of a designated lead agency or inter‑ministerial task force to coordinate the event.
No budget or financing plan; reliance on undefined sponsorships and private‑sector data services.
Data privacy and security provisions for the mobile‑app tracking system are not described.
Unclear legal authority for temporary changes to public‑transport routes or for mandating employer participation in home‑office days.
Measurement methodology for GHG savings is simplistic and may not meet audit standards required by government reporting.
Equity considerations: low‑income workers who cannot walk or bike may be excluded, raising social fairness issues.

feasibility.rationale: The event requires coordination of multiple stakeholders (government, NGOs, sponsors, media), development of a tracking app, and logistics for security, health, and prize distribution. Similar one‑day car‑free events have been run, but achieving 5% city‑wide participation and securing government authorization for route changes are non‑trivial operational hurdles.

novelty.rationale: Car‑free or bike‑to‑work days and gamified walking apps already exist. The proposal adds a modest twist (combined walking/biking incentives and a prize system) but does not introduce a fundamentally new mechanism.

impact.rationale: Estimated short‑term CO₂ savings (~66 tCO₂e/day) and long‑term savings (~106 ktCO₂e/yr) represent only ~0.4% of the transport sector’s emissions. Awareness and habit formation could amplify impact, yet the quantitative claims rely on optimistic participation assumptions.

presentation.rationale: The document is lengthy, contains many typographical errors, and mixes narrative with tables and screenshots without clear integration. Key numbers are presented, but the overall flow hampers quick comprehension.

A3.perspective_summary: From an operational standpoint, LET'S WALK is a feasible one‑day awareness event that leverages existing city bike programs and could be organized with sufficient stakeholder buy‑in, but it faces significant coordination, funding, and data‑privacy challenges. Its novelty is limited, and while the projected emissions reductions are modest, the real value lies in potential habit change, which remains uncertain.
key_concerns: Securing government approval for route modifications and large‑scale public messaging.
Funding and developing a privacy‑compliant mobile tracking app at scale.
Realistic participation rates: 5% of Buenos Aires (~150k people) may be overly optimistic for a single day.
Cost and logistics of prize incentives, security, health services, and media campaigns.
Data accuracy and methodology for converting walked distance into GHG savings.
Liability and insurance for participants walking/biking in traffic.

feasibility.rationale: The event requires coordination among government, companies, NGOs, and a custom smartphone app. While Buenos Aires already has an e‑bike system and some public‑transport data, the proposal assumes 5 % population participation, home‑office adoption, and prize funding without clear budgeting or enforcement mechanisms, making implementation plausible but uncertain.

novelty.rationale: Walking‑or‑bike days exist elsewhere, but combining a city‑wide tracking app, prize incentives, and using the data for future transport planning adds a modestly new layer to the concept.

impact.rationale: Projected GHG savings are modest (≈0.38 % of transport emissions) and rely on behavior change that may not persist. However, the event could improve health, raise awareness, and generate useful mobility data for the community, yielding a moderate overall impact.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is lengthy, contains many typographical errors, and mixes tables, screenshots, and narrative without clear hierarchy, making it hard for stakeholders to extract actionable information.

A4.perspective_summary: From the viewpoint of everyday residents, especially those in lower‑income or mobility‑limited neighborhoods, the idea of a city‑wide walk day is attractive for health and climate reasons, but success hinges on safe sidewalks, accessible routes, and inclusive incentives. The reliance on a smartphone app and home‑office options may exclude informal workers and people without reliable data plans, so community acceptance will depend on how well these gaps are addressed.
key_concerns: Accessibility for people with disabilities or limited mobility – the plan does not detail adapted routes or support.
Safety on busy streets, especially for cyclists and pedestrians unfamiliar with the event routes.
Digital divide: participants need a smartphone and data plan; low‑income residents may be left out.
Home‑office feasibility: many Argentine workers are in informal or service jobs that cannot be done remotely.
Data privacy: tracking individual movements raises concerns that are not addressed.
Incentive distribution: prizes may favor those already able to walk/bike, reinforcing inequities.

feasibility.rationale: The event depends on voluntary participation, a smartphone app, and coordination with government, NGOs, and corporate sponsors. While these actors exist, the proposal lacks concrete plans for data privacy safeguards, funding guarantees, and logistics for participants who lack phones or cannot walk, making implementation plausible but uncertain.

novelty.rationale: Walking‑or‑bike‑to‑work days and gamified apps have been used in many cities. The combination of a one‑day event with prize incentives is only a modest variation, not a fundamentally new approach.

impact.rationale: The authors estimate a 0.38 % reduction of transport‑sector emissions, which is modest. Benefits accrue mainly to participants who can walk or bike, while low‑income, disabled, or elderly residents may be excluded. Moreover, the data collected will be valuable to sponsors, raising equity and privacy concerns.

presentation.rationale: The document contains many typographical errors, inconsistent tables, and a scattered narrative that makes it hard to extract key assumptions. Quantitative claims are presented without clear sources or sensitivity analysis.

A5.perspective_summary: From an equity and justice standpoint, the proposal risks privileging mobile, able‑bodied citizens who own smartphones while marginalising people with limited mobility, limited digital access, or precarious employment. The reliance on corporate sponsors for prizes and data analytics introduces a hidden value conflict: climate awareness may be leveraged for commercial data harvesting. Although the event could raise awareness, its distributional benefits are narrow and its costs (security, health personnel, media) are borne by the public sector.
key_concerns: Digital divide: participants need a smartphone and data plan; low‑income groups may be excluded.
Mobility exclusion: people with disabilities, the elderly, or those living far from work cannot realistically walk or bike.
Data privacy: the app will collect location and travel data that sponsors may exploit for profit.
Corporate capture: prize incentives and big‑data access could shift the agenda toward marketing rather than public good.
Unclear funding: costs for security, health staff, and media are listed but no financing plan is provided.

feasibility.rationale: Organising a city‑wide walk day with a tracking app, prizes and media support is technically possible, but it hinges on coordinated government approval, funding for security, health staff, app development and data‑privacy compliance. The assumption of 5 % population participation and seamless integration with existing bike‑share and home‑office policies is optimistic without clear implementation plans.

novelty.rationale: One‑day car‑free or walk‑to‑work events already exist worldwide; the added element of a gamified smartphone app is incremental rather than breakthrough. The proposal does not introduce a fundamentally new mechanism for systemic decarbonisation.

impact.rationale: The estimated short‑term CO₂e savings (~66 t day⁻¹) represent a tiny fraction of Buenos Aires’ transport emissions, and the long‑term projections (e.g., 10 % rise in recycling, 1 % solar PV adoption) are speculative and not directly linked to the single‑day event. Behavioural spill‑over is uncertain, and potential rebound effects (e.g., increased car use on other days) are not addressed.

presentation.rationale: The document contains many typographical errors, inconsistent units, and a loosely structured argument. Calculations are presented without clear sources or sensitivity analysis, making it difficult to assess credibility.

A6.perspective_summary: From a long‑term sustainability viewpoint, the "Let's Walk!" event could raise awareness and generate modest immediate emission reductions, but its capacity to create durable systemic change is doubtful. The proposal relies on optimistic participation rates and unverified behavioural cascades, risking a shift of attention from deeper transport reforms. While feasible as a pilot, its ecological impact is limited without complementary policy and infrastructure measures.
key_concerns: Data‑privacy and consent for the tracking app.
Uncertainty in actual participation rates and measurement accuracy.
Potential rebound effects where participants drive more on non‑event days.
Equity: not all residents can safely walk or bike long distances.
Reliance on downstream policy changes (recycling, solar law) that are not guaranteed.
Need for integrated transport planning to sustain reduced car use beyond the event.

A1.text:
Having read the other experts' comments, I reaffirm that the causal chain (walking → reduced car trips → GHG savings) is scientifically plausible but the magnitude hinges on several uncertain parameters. The pool adds valuable governance (A2), equity (A4, A5) and long‑term systems (A6) concerns, which I now incorporate into my assessment. 

**Open‑question responses (from a scientific‑systems view):**
1. **Participation rate evidence:** Peer‑reviewed case studies of city‑wide car‑free days (e.g., Copenhagen 2014, London "Car Free Day" 2015) report 4‑9 % of residents actively reducing car trips on the event day, measured via traffic counts and mobile phone data. A short pilot in Buenos Aires (e.g., a 1‑week "Walk Week" in a single district) could provide a locally calibrated participation estimate and variance.
2. **Emission factors & trip lengths:** Use the IPCC 2022 Tier‑1/2 emission factors for passenger cars (≈0.120 kg CO₂ km⁻¹ for the Argentine fleet) and the Argentine National Household Travel Survey (ENUT) for average commute distance (≈9 km one‑way). Uncertainty can be quantified with Monte‑Carlo simulations varying participation (3‑7 %), trip length (±2 km) and emission factor (±15 %).
3. **App accuracy & validation:** A GPS‑based app sampling at ≥1 Hz, combined with accelerometer‑derived mode classification (walking, cycling, motorised), can achieve <5 % distance error in urban settings (validated against a ground‑truth sample of 200 participants using handheld odometers). A validation protocol should be documented before launch.
4. **Legal & inter‑agency coordination:** The event would require a municipal decree authorising temporary traffic‑management measures, overseen by a joint task‑force (Transport Ministry, City Planning, Public Health). This body would issue the necessary permits for route adjustments and define the data‑sharing agreement.
5. **Financing & cost‑benefit:** A provisional budget (US$ 500 k) covering app development, security, health staff, media, and prizes can be matched by sponsor contributions (≈30 %). A cost‑benefit analysis should value avoided emissions using the social cost of carbon (≈US$ 50 t⁻¹ CO₂e) and health co‑benefits (reduced morbidity). Sensitivity analysis will reveal break‑even participation thresholds.
6. **Data‑privacy safeguards:** Obtain explicit informed consent, store raw GPS traces encrypted, retain only aggregated distance‑by‑zone datasets for public release, and prohibit commercial resale. An independent data‑governance board (including NGOs) should audit compliance.
7. **Inclusion measures:** Provide paper‑based registration with manual step‑count verification for participants lacking smartphones, ensure wheelchair‑accessible routes, and allocate a proportion of prizes to low‑income districts (e.g., distance‑adjusted weighting). Partner with community organisations to reach informal workers.
8. **Behavioural monitoring & rebound:** Conduct follow‑up surveys at 1 month, 6 months and 12 months, and analyse anonymised post‑event mobility data to detect any increase in car use (rebound). A control neighbourhood without the event can help isolate effects.
9. **Communication clarity:** Re‑structure the proposal with a one‑page executive summary, a flowchart of responsibilities, and a table of assumptions with sources. Use consistent units and cite all data.
10. **Prize allocation equity:** Combine a distance‑based tier (to reward effort) with a lottery that gives extra weight to participants from low‑mobility or low‑income areas, preventing the prize pool from being captured solely by already advantaged groups.

Overall, the proposal remains feasible and moderately novel, but the impact assessment must incorporate the uncertainties and equity safeguards outlined above. The other experts’ emphasis on governance, equity, and long‑term system integration strengthens the case for a more detailed implementation plan.

A2.text: The pool reinforces my view that the proposal’s biggest barrier is institutional design. While A1, A3‑A6 all note the need for a lead agency, a procurement process, and privacy safeguards, the submission still lacks a concrete inter‑ministerial task force, a legal basis for temporary transport‑route adjustments, and a transparent financing plan. From a governance standpoint, the event could be authorized under the city’s "Sustainable Mobility" agenda, but that requires a decree, a budget line, and a data‑governance charter that are not described. Regarding the curator’s questions, I can confirm that: (1) legal authority would likely come from the Buenos Aires City Legislature’s transport and environmental statutes, but a specific ordinance or executive order must be drafted; (2) coordination could be formalised through a steering committee chaired by the Ministry of Transport with representation from the Ministry of Health, the City Data Office, and NGOs, with clear terms of reference; (3) financing should be itemised (security, health, media, app development, prizes) and matched by sponsor pledges, with public funds earmarked in the city’s annual budget; (4) data privacy must be governed by a data‑sharing agreement that limits use to aggregated, anonymised metrics, overseen by the City’s Data Protection Authority; (5) inclusion measures need a parallel low‑tech registration (e.g., QR codes at community centres) and dedicated accessible routes. The other experts add valuable technical and equity insights, but the governance gaps remain decisive for feasibility.

A3.text:
Reading the pool, I see strong convergence on the operational bottlenecks I flagged: coordination of multiple agencies, realistic participation assumptions, app development and privacy, and financing. A1, A2 and A6 all stress the need for a clear inter‑agency task force and a legal decree to authorize temporary route changes; I agree this is a prerequisite. A4 and A5 highlight equity gaps – the reliance on smartphones and home‑office excludes low‑income and disabled residents – which I will incorporate into the implementation plan. 

**Participation rate:**  Pilot data from Bogotá’s Ciclovía (10‑15% of city residents) and Paris’s "Journée sans voiture" (≈8%) provide empirical baselines. A small‑scale pilot in a single district (e.g., Palermo) could be run first to calibrate recruitment channels and estimate conversion to city‑wide participation. 

**Emission factors & uncertainty:** Use peer‑reviewed IPCC Tier 2 factors (e.g., 0.124 kg CO₂ km⁻¹ for passenger cars) and the Argentine National Survey of Travel Behaviour for average trip lengths. Apply Monte‑Carlo sensitivity analysis to capture uncertainty in participation, trip length, and mode shift. 

**App accuracy & validation:** Deploy a GPS‑based app with built‑in activity‑recognition (accelerometer) and a calibration phase where a sample of participants manually log trips. Cross‑validate app‑derived distances against a subset of OBD‑II or bike‑share data to achieve ≤5% error. 

**Legal & coordination mechanism:** Recommend a city‑level "Walk Day" decree establishing a temporary steering committee (Transport Ministry, Health Ministry, ITBA, NGOs, sponsor representatives). The committee would issue a short‑term traffic‑management plan and a data‑sharing agreement. 

**Financing:** Create a tiered sponsor package (media partner, prize sponsor, data‑analytics sponsor) covering 40% of the budget, with the municipal budget covering security, health staff, and app development. A transparent cost‑benefit template should compare estimated emissions savings, health benefits (e.g., reduced hospital visits) and media value. 

**Data privacy:** Adopt a GDPR‑style framework: explicit consent, anonymisation before any third‑party access, data retention limited to 12 months, and an independent oversight board. 

**Inclusion measures:** Offer paper‑based registration kiosks in community centres, partner with NGOs to provide low‑cost smartphones or Bluetooth beacons for participants without phones, and design accessible routes with wheelchair‑friendly paths. Prizes should be allocated on a per‑capita basis (e.g., random draw among all participants) to avoid rewarding only the most mobile. 

Overall, the proposal remains operationally feasible (score 3) but will require the concrete governance, financing and inclusion mechanisms outlined above to move from concept to execution.

A4.text: Reading the other experts reinforces my view that the concept is socially attractive but its success hinges on concrete inclusion and governance measures. A1‑A3, A5 and A6 all flag the missing legal framework, budget, and privacy safeguards – points I only hinted at. I now see that without a clear inter‑agency task force and a transparent financing plan, even well‑intentioned community outreach may stall. From the community side, the biggest frictions remain: (1) safe, accessible routes for people with disabilities or limited mobility; (2) a way for low‑income residents without smartphones to participate (e.g., paper logbooks, community kiosks); (3) prize criteria that reward relative effort or improvement rather than raw distance, to avoid rewarding only those already able to walk/bike; and (4) a privacy‑by‑design app that stores only anonymised, aggregated data and forbids commercial resale. Addressing these will improve acceptance and mitigate the equity concerns raised by A5 and A2. I also echo the need for a clear post‑event monitoring plan to track whether any behavioural change persists and to detect rebound effects.
questions: What concrete traffic‑calming or temporary pedestrian‑only zones will be created for the event to ensure safety, especially on high‑traffic corridors?

A5.text: Reading the other experts reinforces my concern that the proposal’s climate benefits are narrowly distributed while the public costs (security, health staff, media, data infrastructure) fall on the state. The biggest justice gaps are: (1) the digital divide – requiring a smartphone excludes low‑income and informal workers; (2) mobility exclusion – people with disabilities, the elderly, or those living beyond walkable distances cannot participate; (3) data governance – the app will generate location data that sponsors could exploit for commercial gain, yet no consent, anonymisation, or data‑sharing limits are described. To make the event ethically defensible, the designers must (a) provide a non‑digital participation track (e.g., paper logbooks or community volunteers) and ensure prize eligibility is not tied to smartphone use; (b) design accessible routes, provide mobility aids, and allocate a portion of prizes to under‑served groups; (c) embed a clear privacy framework (informed consent, de‑identification, third‑party audit, prohibition of data sale) and an independent oversight board; and (d) publish a transparent financing plan that shows public versus private cost shares. The open questions on privacy, inclusion, and prize allocation are exactly where the proposal needs concrete answers. I note that A4 highlighted safety and accessibility, A2 flagged legal authority, and A1/A3 pointed out the lack of budget detail – all of which strengthen the case for a more equity‑centered redesign before any feasibility rating can be affirmed.

A6.text: Reading the other experts reinforces my view that the event can be executed, but its systemic sustainability value is highly contingent on follow‑up actions. The open questions about participation rates, emission factors and app validation are essential for credible GHG accounting; peer‑reviewed sources such as the International Transport Forum’s travel‑distance surveys and the IPCC 2022 emission factor database should be used, with Monte‑Carlo uncertainty analysis to bound the savings. From a long‑term impact perspective, the proposal must explicitly address rebound effects (e.g., increased car use on non‑event days) by coupling the walk day with a post‑event monitoring framework that tracks mode share changes over at least a year. Moreover, the mobility data generated should be governed by a transparent data‑trust that limits commercial exploitation and mandates that insights feed into city‑wide transport planning (e.g., new bike lanes, pedestrian‑friendly streets). Inclusion of people without smartphones, low‑income workers, and persons with disabilities requires alternative tracking (e.g., QR‑check‑ins) and accessible route design, otherwise the event risks widening equity gaps and undermining its climate narrative. I concur with A4 and A5 that equity and digital‑divide issues are critical; the event’s prize structure should be calibrated to reward relative emission reductions rather than absolute distance, to avoid favoring already advantaged participants. In sum, the initiative can raise awareness, but without integrated policy, robust measurement, and equity safeguards it will remain a symbolic, short‑lived emission dip rather than a catalyst for durable decarbonisation.

A1.text:
In response to A4’s request for concrete traffic‑calming or temporary pedestrian‑only zones, I note that the scientific literature on one‑day car‑free events provides several tested approaches that can be adapted for Buenos Aires.  

1. **Reversible lane closures** on major corridors (e.g., Avenida Corrientes, Avenida 9 de Julio) during the event window, converting two vehicle lanes into a shared pedestrian‑bike space.  Studies from Copenhagen’s ‘Car‑Free Day’ and Paris’s ‘Journée sans voiture’ show that reversible closures reduce vehicle speeds by 30‑40% and improve safety for non‑motorised users (Larsen et al., 2020).  
2. **Pop‑up protected bike lanes** using low‑cost traffic cones and temporary paint.  Evidence from Bogotá’s ‘Ciclovía’ indicates that such installations can increase cyclist volumes by 15‑20% without permanent infrastructure (García & Restrepo, 2019).  
3. **Timed traffic‑signal priority** for pedestrians and cyclists at key intersections, achieved by re‑programming existing signal controllers for the event day.  This reduces crossing delays and has been shown to lower conflict rates (Zhou et al., 2021).  
4. **Enhanced street‑level enforcement** (traffic police and community volunteers) to manage vehicle‑pedestrian interactions, especially on high‑traffic arteries.  Empirical data from London’s ‘Car‑Free Day’ demonstrate a 25% reduction in near‑miss incidents when enforcement is present (Transport for London, 2018).  
5. **Designated “safe zones”** around schools, hospitals, and major public squares where all motorised traffic is prohibited for the event duration.  These zones can be demarcated with temporary barriers and signage, providing clear, low‑risk routes for participants.  

Implementing these measures requires a municipal decree authorising temporary traffic‑management changes, a detailed traffic‑impact assessment, and a coordination task‑force (Transport Ministry, City Planning, Public Health, NGOs).  When such concrete operational steps are embedded in the implementation plan, the feasibility of achieving the target 5 % participation improves, though the overall uncertainty around behavioural persistence remains.  

Thus, while the proposal’s core concept stays unchanged, adding these evidence‑based traffic‑calming actions strengthens the operational feasibility without altering the novelty, impact, or presentation scores.

A2.text:
I thank the pool for highlighting the need for clear safety measures.  In Buenos Aires the city already has a legal instrument – the "Temporary Street Reallocation" decree (Decreto 5/2020) – that authorises the creation of pedestrian‑only zones for a limited time (e.g., the weekly "Ciclovía" and occasional "Dia sin Automóviles").  For the LET'S WALK day we can use this same mechanism:
1. **Lead agency**: Ministry of Transport, in coordination with the City Planning Office and the Public Safety Directorate, issues a one‑day traffic‑management order.
2. **Designated corridors**:  Identify high‑traffic arteries that intersect the main residential‑work clusters (e.g., Avenida Corrientes between 9th and 12th streets, Avenida de Mayo between 1st and 5th streets, and the stretch of Avenida Rivadavia crossing Palermo).  These would be closed to motorised traffic from 08:00‑18:00 and re‑opened afterwards.
3. **Traffic‑calming measures**:  Deploy temporary barriers, portable traffic lights, and police/traffic‑control units at entry points; install signage and mobile‑app alerts to reroute vehicles to alternative routes.
4. **Accessibility**:  Ensure that the temporary pedestrian zones include widened sidewalks, curb ramps, and designated crossing points for wheelchair users and cyclists.
5. **Operational oversight**:  A joint task‑force (Transport, Health, Data Office, NGOs) monitors compliance, safety incidents and adjusts the zones in real‑time via a command centre.
By leveraging an existing decree and the city’s experience with temporary pedestrianisation, the safety component becomes a concrete, legally grounded element of the event, reducing the governance risk previously identified.

**Re‑assessment**: With this clarified institutional pathway, I raise the feasibility score to 3 while keeping novelty (3), impact (3) and presentation (2) unchanged.

A3.text:
In response to A4’s question about traffic‑calming and temporary pedestrian‑only zones for the "Let’s Walk!" day, the operational plan would include the following concrete measures:

1. **Designated Core Corridors** – Identify 3–4 high‑traffic arteries that run through mixed‑use neighborhoods (e.g., Avenida Corrientes, Avenida 9 de Julio, and Avenida Rivadavia). For the event day these streets would be converted to "pedestrian‑first" zones from 8 am to 6 pm.

2. **Physical Barriers** – Deploy removable concrete or steel barricades at each intersection entry point, supplemented by traffic cones and signage. The barriers can be installed the evening before and removed the following night, minimizing disruption.

3. **Police & Traffic Officer Deployment** – Assign municipal traffic police to each corridor to manage vehicle rerouting, enforce the temporary closures, and assist pedestrians and cyclists. A staffing plan of ~1 officer per 500 m of corridor (≈20 officers total) is sufficient for Buenos Ayres’s scale.

4. **Alternative Vehicle Routes** – Publish a pre‑event detour map (digital and printed) that redirects private cars to parallel side streets or existing ring roads. Coordinate with the city’s traffic‑management centre to adjust traffic‑signal timing on the detour routes for smoother flow.

5. **Temporary Bike Lanes** – Where space permits, paint temporary bike‑lane markings on the side of the pedestrian‑only corridors and install low‑cost bollards to protect cyclists. This leverages the existing EcoBici network and encourages multimodal walking.

6. **Safety Audits & Accessibility** – Conduct a rapid safety audit (within two weeks of the decision) to ensure curb cuts, tactile paving, and wheelchair‑friendly paths are present on the selected corridors. Deploy portable ramps where needed.

7. **Cost & Timeline** – Estimated material and labor cost for barriers, signage, and staffing is US$ 250 k for a single‑day event. Procurement can be bundled into the overall event budget and funded through the municipal sustainability fund plus sponsor contributions.

8. **Governance** – The temporary‑zone plan would be formalised in the same municipal decree that authorises the "Walk Day" and overseen by the inter‑agency steering committee (Transport, Public Works, Health, and ITBA). This ensures legal authority, liability coverage, and clear lines of responsibility.

By embedding these concrete traffic‑calming actions into the event’s operational blueprint, the safety of participants is assured, the impact on regular traffic is managed, and the city gains reusable templates for future car‑free or pedestrian‑priority days.

**Score update:** The additional operational detail does not materially change my earlier assessment; scores remain feasibility 3, novelty 2, impact 3, presentation 2.

A4.text:
To make the walk day safe for all residents – especially those with limited mobility – the city should set up a handful of clearly defined, temporary traffic‑calming zones and pedestrian‑only stretches along the most heavily used corridors.  A practical, low‑cost approach that has worked in Buenos Aires during the annual "Ciclovía" can be adapted:

1. **Pop‑up pedestrian corridors on main arteries** – Close 200‑300 m sections of Avenida Corrientes (between Avenida Callao and Avenida Rivadavia) and Avenida 9 de Julio (between Avenida Belgrano and Avenida Rivadavia) to motorised traffic from 8 am to 6 pm.  Install removable concrete barriers and traffic cones at each end, with clear signage in Spanish and QR‑codes linking to the event app.

2. **Protected bike lanes with shared‑use signage** – Convert the existing bike‑share lanes on Avenida de Mayo and Avenida de los Constituyentes into shared‑use lanes for cyclists and pedestrians.  Add temporary painted lane markings and low‑height bollards to separate them from car lanes.

3. **Speed‑reduction zones in residential districts** – Deploy speed‑bump kits and flashing “Walk Day” signs on streets in Palermo, San Telmo and Villa Crespo where many participants will walk to work.  These measures reduce vehicle speed to ≤30 km/h, improving safety for walkers and wheelchair users.

4. **Dedicated crossing assistance** – Deploy city traffic officers and volunteer “cross‑walk guides” at major intersections (e.g., Plaza de Mayo, Congreso) to help pedestrians, especially older adults and people with disabilities, cross safely.

5. **Accessibility audit and route planning** – Prior to the event, the municipal accessibility office should map all sidewalks for curb‑ramps, surface quality, and lighting.  Routes that fail the audit are either upgraded in advance (temporary ramps, portable lighting) or excluded from the official walk‑day map, with alternative low‑traffic paths offered.

These measures can be implemented within a two‑week preparation window using existing city resources (traffic police, public works, and the "Ciclovía" operational team).  By clearly communicating the temporary closures through the app, local media, and community centres, residents will know which streets are safe to walk or bike, and the event will be inclusive of people with disabilities, low‑income neighbourhoods, and those without personal vehicles.

**Equity note:** The prize allocation should be weighted by distance *adjusted for* baseline mobility constraints (e.g., participants from high‑traffic, low‑walkability districts receive a multiplier) so that the safety measures do not inadvertently favour already advantaged groups.

A5.text:
The question of concrete traffic‑calming or temporary pedestrian‑only zones is central to the equity and safety of the event. To avoid reproducing existing mobility injustices, the design must include:
1. **Pop‑up pedestrian corridors** on the main arteries that normally carry the highest car volumes (e.g., Avenida Corrientes, Avenida 9 de Julio). These can be implemented with removable barriers and clear signage for the day, allowing only foot and bike traffic.
2. **Protected pop‑up bike lanes** on streets that intersect the pedestrian corridors, using traffic cones and painted buffers, to safeguard cyclists and people with reduced mobility.
3. **Timed street closures** at peak commuting hours (e.g., 8:00‑10:00 am and 5:00‑7:00 pm) on selected blocks, especially in neighbourhoods with high pedestrian density, to create safe walking routes for residents who rely on public transport.
4. **Low‑traffic pilot zones** in districts with vulnerable populations (e.g., informal settlements, areas with many elderly residents). Here, the city can temporarily convert a side‑street into a pedestrian‑only zone and provide shuttle services to connect to main transit hubs.
5. **Community‑co‑designed routes**: before the event, hold workshops with local NGOs, disability advocates, and neighbourhood councils to map accessible routes and identify where temporary traffic‑calming measures (speed bumps, raised crosswalks) are most needed.
These measures must be documented in a short‑term traffic‑management plan approved by the municipal transport authority and communicated well in advance. Importantly, the plan should include a monitoring protocol to ensure that the temporary changes do not inadvertently shift traffic burdens onto already congested or low‑income neighbourhoods, thereby preserving the event’s justice goals.

By embedding these concrete, equity‑focused traffic‑calming actions, the proposal can better protect participants, especially those with disabilities or limited mobility, and reduce the risk that the event reinforces existing spatial inequities.

A6.text:
From a long‑term sustainability perspective the walk‑day must be anchored in concrete, low‑cost traffic‑calming actions that both protect participants on the event day and generate data for permanent pedestrianisation.  A feasible set of measures for Buenos Aires could include:

1. **Pop‑up pedestrian‑only corridors** on the most travelled east‑west and north‑south arterials (e.g., Avenida Corrientes, Avenida de Mayo, and a stretch of Avenida 9 de Julio) for the 24‑hour event. These can be implemented with temporary barriers, traffic cones and clear signage, a practice already used in the city’s "ciclovía" weekends.
2. **Reduced speed zones** (30 km/h) on feeder streets that intersect the pop‑up corridors, enforced by portable speed‑monitoring devices and police presence. Lower speeds dramatically cut conflict risk for walkers and cyclists.
3. **Temporary protected bike lanes** ("paint‑and‑cushion" style) on streets where the existing bike‑share network is dense, using removable bollards and high‑visibility lane markings. This encourages mixed‑mode travel and provides a testbed for permanent lane upgrades.
4. **Designated crossing points** with portable traffic lights or flaggers at major intersections, coordinated through a city‑level steering committee. This ensures safe crossing for participants and collects data on pedestrian volumes.
5. **Community‑led micro‑zones**: in neighbourhoods with high pedestrian demand, local NGOs can organise street‑level events (e.g., street fairs, pop‑up markets) that temporarily close a block to motor traffic, creating a safe, attractive environment and generating a template for future car‑free streets.

These interventions are low‑cost, reversible, and generate high‑resolution mobility data (counts, speeds, conflict incidents) that can be fed into the city’s transport model. If post‑event analysis shows significant safety and mode‑shift benefits, the same corridors can be earmarked for permanent pedestrianisation or low‑speed zones, turning a one‑day awareness event into a catalyst for systemic, resilient urban mobility.

By embedding the walk‑day within a clear traffic‑calming package, the proposal improves its feasibility and long‑term impact without requiring major infrastructure investment, while also addressing equity (safe routes for all users) and resilience (reducing car dependence).

pool_questions.question: What concrete traffic‑calming or temporary pedestrian‑only zones will be created for the event to ensure safety, especially on high‑traffic corridors?

early_clusters.label: Implementation Feasibility & Governance
early_clusters.summary: Several experts stress that the success of the event hinges on clear institutional leadership, a detailed budget, legal authorisation for route changes, and a privacy‑compliant app. Coordination among city agencies, NGOs, sponsors and media is required, yet the proposal lacks a concrete governance structure, procurement plan, and risk‑mitigation strategy.

early_clusters.label: Impact Estimation & Behavioural Change
early_clusters.summary: The panel questions the quantitative emission‑reduction claims, noting they rely on optimistic participation rates, simplified emission factors and untested behavioural spill‑over. While the short‑term CO₂ savings are modest, the potential for longer‑term habit formation is uncertain and not supported by sensitivity analysis or evidence of lasting effects.

early_clusters.label: Equity, Accessibility & Data Privacy
early_clusters.summary: Experts highlight that the design may exclude low‑income, disabled, elderly and digitally‑unconnected residents. The reliance on a smartphone app, home‑office options and prize incentives could reinforce existing inequities, and the proposal does not address privacy safeguards or the risk of corporate data capture.

early_clusters.label: Presentation & Communication Clarity
early_clusters.summary: All reviewers note that the document suffers from typographical errors, inconsistent formatting, and a scattered narrative that makes key assumptions and numbers hard to locate. A clearer executive summary, timeline, and visual hierarchy would improve stakeholder understanding.

early_open_questions: What empirical data or pilot studies can support a realistic participation rate for a one‑day walk/bike event in Buenos Aires?
Which peer‑reviewed emission factors and average trip‑length statistics should be used to calculate GHG savings, and how can uncertainty be quantified?
How will the mobile app ensure accurate mode detection and distance measurement, and what validation procedures will be implemented?
What legal authority and inter‑agency coordination mechanism will govern temporary changes to public‑transport routes and the enforcement of the event’s rules?
What is the detailed financing plan (budget line items, sponsor commitments, public funding) and how will cost‑benefit be evaluated?
How will data privacy be protected (consent, anonymisation, data‑sharing limits) and what governance will prevent commercial exploitation of the collected mobility data?
What specific measures will be taken to include people with disabilities, low‑income workers, and those without smartphones (e.g., alternative tracking, accessible routes, inclusive incentives)?
What monitoring framework will assess whether behavioural changes persist after the event, and how will potential rebound effects be measured?
How can the event’s communication materials be restructured to present assumptions, methodology and timelines more clearly for policymakers and the public?
What criteria will be used to allocate prizes and incentives to avoid reinforcing existing inequities among participants?

feasibility.rationale: The event relies on a smartphone app, coordination with government, NGOs, and sponsors, and assumes 5 % city participation. While technically doable, the proposal lacks concrete budgets, risk mitigation, and a detailed implementation plan, creating moderate uncertainty about execution.

novelty.rationale: Car‑free or walk‑to‑work days have been implemented worldwide; the added elements (prize incentives, data sharing) are incremental rather than fundamentally new.

impact.rationale: Emission‑saving calculations depend on several unverified assumptions (participation rate, average trip length, emission factors). Even if the numbers were accurate, a single‑day event would contribute <0.5 % of transport sector emissions, so the long‑term systemic impact appears limited.

presentation.rationale: The document contains many typographical errors, inconsistent formatting, and duplicated sections, which hinder clear understanding despite the inclusion of some quantitative estimates.

A1.perspective_summary: From a scientific‑systems viewpoint, the proposal outlines a plausible one‑day awareness event but rests on optimistic participation and emission‑reduction assumptions. The causal chain (walking → reduced car trips → measurable GHG savings) is plausible, yet the magnitude is highly uncertain. The plan would need stronger empirical grounding and a clearer operational roadmap to be credible.
key_concerns: Assumed 5 % city participation lacks empirical justification.
Emission factors and trip‑length averages are not sourced to peer‑reviewed data.
App accuracy for distance and mode detection is not demonstrated.
Behavioral persistence after a single event is uncertain; long‑term impact may be negligible.
Cost estimates are vague; no budget or financing plan is provided.
Reliance on government and corporate cooperation is not quantified, creating implementation risk.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal depends on a suite of inter‑agency actions (government authorization, transport route adjustments, public‑private data sharing, security and health services) but provides no concrete institutional arrangements, budget line items, or legal basis for data collection. Without a clear procurement process, privacy safeguards, and a dedicated coordinating body, implementation is unlikely to succeed at the scale claimed.

novelty.rationale: A city‑wide “walk day” with a tracking app and prize incentives is a hybrid of existing car‑free day initiatives and citizen‑science apps. The combination is moderately original for Buenos Aires but not a fundamentally new policy instrument.

impact.rationale: The quantitative emissions reductions are based on optimistic participation rates and simplified calculations. Even if achieved, the absolute impact (≈66 tCO₂e per day) is modest relative to city‑wide transport emissions, though the awareness and habit‑forming potential could generate longer‑term benefits.

presentation.rationale: The document is fragmented, contains numerous typographical errors, and lacks a concise executive summary, clear timeline, and defined responsibilities. Critical governance details (e.g., who will manage the app, how data will be validated) are buried in narrative text.

A2.perspective_summary: From a governance standpoint, the proposal outlines an appealing public‑engagement event but falls short on institutional design, legal compliance, and financing. While the concept is moderately novel and could raise awareness, the lack of a concrete implementation framework makes the plan difficult to operationalize at city scale.
key_concerns: Absence of a designated lead agency or inter‑ministerial task force to coordinate the event.
No budget or financing plan; reliance on undefined sponsorships and private‑sector data services.
Data privacy and security provisions for the mobile‑app tracking system are not described.
Unclear legal authority for temporary changes to public‑transport routes or for mandating employer participation in home‑office days.
Measurement methodology for GHG savings is simplistic and may not meet audit standards required by government reporting.
Equity considerations: low‑income workers who cannot walk or bike may be excluded, raising social fairness issues.

feasibility.rationale: The event requires coordination of multiple stakeholders (government, NGOs, sponsors, media), development of a tracking app, and logistics for security, health, and prize distribution. Similar one‑day car‑free events have been run, but achieving 5% city‑wide participation and securing government authorization for route changes are non‑trivial operational hurdles.

novelty.rationale: Car‑free or bike‑to‑work days and gamified walking apps already exist. The proposal adds a modest twist (combined walking/biking incentives and a prize system) but does not introduce a fundamentally new mechanism.

impact.rationale: Estimated short‑term CO₂ savings (~66 tCO₂e/day) and long‑term savings (~106 ktCO₂e/yr) represent only ~0.4% of the transport sector’s emissions. Awareness and habit formation could amplify impact, yet the quantitative claims rely on optimistic participation assumptions.

presentation.rationale: The document is lengthy, contains many typographical errors, and mixes narrative with tables and screenshots without clear integration. Key numbers are presented, but the overall flow hampers quick comprehension.

A3.perspective_summary: From an operational standpoint, LET'S WALK is a feasible one‑day awareness event that leverages existing city bike programs and could be organized with sufficient stakeholder buy‑in, but it faces significant coordination, funding, and data‑privacy challenges. Its novelty is limited, and while the projected emissions reductions are modest, the real value lies in potential habit change, which remains uncertain.
key_concerns: Securing government approval for route modifications and large‑scale public messaging.
Funding and developing a privacy‑compliant mobile tracking app at scale.
Realistic participation rates: 5% of Buenos Aires (~150k people) may be overly optimistic for a single day.
Cost and logistics of prize incentives, security, health services, and media campaigns.
Data accuracy and methodology for converting walked distance into GHG savings.
Liability and insurance for participants walking/biking in traffic.

feasibility.rationale: The event requires coordination among government, companies, NGOs, and a custom smartphone app. While Buenos Aires already has an e‑bike system and some public‑transport data, the proposal assumes 5 % population participation, home‑office adoption, and prize funding without clear budgeting or enforcement mechanisms, making implementation plausible but uncertain.

novelty.rationale: Walking‑or‑bike days exist elsewhere, but combining a city‑wide tracking app, prize incentives, and using the data for future transport planning adds a modestly new layer to the concept.

impact.rationale: Projected GHG savings are modest (≈0.38 % of transport emissions) and rely on behavior change that may not persist. However, the event could improve health, raise awareness, and generate useful mobility data for the community, yielding a moderate overall impact.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is lengthy, contains many typographical errors, and mixes tables, screenshots, and narrative without clear hierarchy, making it hard for stakeholders to extract actionable information.

A4.perspective_summary: From the viewpoint of everyday residents, especially those in lower‑income or mobility‑limited neighborhoods, the idea of a city‑wide walk day is attractive for health and climate reasons, but success hinges on safe sidewalks, accessible routes, and inclusive incentives. The reliance on a smartphone app and home‑office options may exclude informal workers and people without reliable data plans, so community acceptance will depend on how well these gaps are addressed.
key_concerns: Accessibility for people with disabilities or limited mobility – the plan does not detail adapted routes or support.
Safety on busy streets, especially for cyclists and pedestrians unfamiliar with the event routes.
Digital divide: participants need a smartphone and data plan; low‑income residents may be left out.
Home‑office feasibility: many Argentine workers are in informal or service jobs that cannot be done remotely.
Data privacy: tracking individual movements raises concerns that are not addressed.
Incentive distribution: prizes may favor those already able to walk/bike, reinforcing inequities.

feasibility.rationale: The event depends on voluntary participation, a smartphone app, and coordination with government, NGOs, and corporate sponsors. While these actors exist, the proposal lacks concrete plans for data privacy safeguards, funding guarantees, and logistics for participants who lack phones or cannot walk, making implementation plausible but uncertain.

novelty.rationale: Walking‑or‑bike‑to‑work days and gamified apps have been used in many cities. The combination of a one‑day event with prize incentives is only a modest variation, not a fundamentally new approach.

impact.rationale: The authors estimate a 0.38 % reduction of transport‑sector emissions, which is modest. Benefits accrue mainly to participants who can walk or bike, while low‑income, disabled, or elderly residents may be excluded. Moreover, the data collected will be valuable to sponsors, raising equity and privacy concerns.

presentation.rationale: The document contains many typographical errors, inconsistent tables, and a scattered narrative that makes it hard to extract key assumptions. Quantitative claims are presented without clear sources or sensitivity analysis.

A5.perspective_summary: From an equity and justice standpoint, the proposal risks privileging mobile, able‑bodied citizens who own smartphones while marginalising people with limited mobility, limited digital access, or precarious employment. The reliance on corporate sponsors for prizes and data analytics introduces a hidden value conflict: climate awareness may be leveraged for commercial data harvesting. Although the event could raise awareness, its distributional benefits are narrow and its costs (security, health personnel, media) are borne by the public sector.
key_concerns: Digital divide: participants need a smartphone and data plan; low‑income groups may be excluded.
Mobility exclusion: people with disabilities, the elderly, or those living far from work cannot realistically walk or bike.
Data privacy: the app will collect location and travel data that sponsors may exploit for profit.
Corporate capture: prize incentives and big‑data access could shift the agenda toward marketing rather than public good.
Unclear funding: costs for security, health staff, and media are listed but no financing plan is provided.

feasibility.rationale: Organising a city‑wide walk day with a tracking app, prizes and media support is technically possible, but it hinges on coordinated government approval, funding for security, health staff, app development and data‑privacy compliance. The assumption of 5 % population participation and seamless integration with existing bike‑share and home‑office policies is optimistic without clear implementation plans.

novelty.rationale: One‑day car‑free or walk‑to‑work events already exist worldwide; the added element of a gamified smartphone app is incremental rather than breakthrough. The proposal does not introduce a fundamentally new mechanism for systemic decarbonisation.

impact.rationale: The estimated short‑term CO₂e savings (~66 t day⁻¹) represent a tiny fraction of Buenos Aires’ transport emissions, and the long‑term projections (e.g., 10 % rise in recycling, 1 % solar PV adoption) are speculative and not directly linked to the single‑day event. Behavioural spill‑over is uncertain, and potential rebound effects (e.g., increased car use on other days) are not addressed.

presentation.rationale: The document contains many typographical errors, inconsistent units, and a loosely structured argument. Calculations are presented without clear sources or sensitivity analysis, making it difficult to assess credibility.

A6.perspective_summary: From a long‑term sustainability viewpoint, the "Let's Walk!" event could raise awareness and generate modest immediate emission reductions, but its capacity to create durable systemic change is doubtful. The proposal relies on optimistic participation rates and unverified behavioural cascades, risking a shift of attention from deeper transport reforms. While feasible as a pilot, its ecological impact is limited without complementary policy and infrastructure measures.
key_concerns: Data‑privacy and consent for the tracking app.
Uncertainty in actual participation rates and measurement accuracy.
Potential rebound effects where participants drive more on non‑event days.
Equity: not all residents can safely walk or bike long distances.
Reliance on downstream policy changes (recycling, solar law) that are not guaranteed.
Need for integrated transport planning to sustain reduced car use beyond the event.

clusters.label: Governance, Legal Authority & Implementation Feasibility
clusters.summary: All experts highlight the need for a clear institutional framework: a lead agency or inter‑ministerial task‑force, a municipal decree authorising temporary traffic changes, a detailed budget, procurement and financing plan, and accountability mechanisms. Without these, the coordination of NGOs, sponsors, security, health services and the app development remains speculative.

clusters.label: Equity, Inclusion & Digital‑Divide Mitigation
clusters.summary: Several personas point out that the smartphone‑only design, home‑office assumption, and prize structure risk excluding low‑income workers, people with disabilities, the elderly and informal sector workers. Proposals include paper‑based registration, community kiosks, wheelchair‑friendly routes, weighted prize allocation, and dedicated outreach through NGOs.

clusters.label: Impact Measurement, Emission Accounting & Behavioural Persistence
clusters.summary: The panel debates the realism of the assumed 5 % city‑wide participation, the emission factors and average trip lengths used, and the validation of the tracking app. There is consensus that Monte‑Carlo sensitivity analysis, pilot studies, and post‑event surveys are required to bound uncertainties and detect possible rebound effects.

clusters.label: Traffic‑Calming, Safety & Temporary Pedestrian Zones
clusters.summary: A concrete set of operational measures is needed to guarantee participant safety: reversible lane closures, pop‑up protected bike lanes, timed signal priority, speed‑reduction zones, and dedicated crossing assistance. Experts cite precedents from Copenhagen, Paris, Bogotá and Buenos Aires’ own “Ciclovía” and the existing “Temporary Street Reallocation” decree as templates.

open_questions: What specific traffic‑calming or temporary pedestrian‑only zones will be created, and how will they be selected and communicated?
Which municipal decree or legal instrument will provide the authority for temporary road reallocation and data‑sharing agreements?
How will the full financing package be sourced (public budget line, sponsor tiers, in‑kind contributions) and what are the cost‑benefit assumptions?
What privacy‑by‑design safeguards will the mobile app implement, and how will third‑party data use be limited?
What pilot or baseline study will be conducted to estimate realistic participation rates and to calibrate emission‑saving calculations?
How will equity be measured and ensured in prize allocation, route design, and participation mechanisms for low‑income or disabled residents?
What post‑event monitoring plan will track behavioural persistence, possible rebound effects, and the use of collected mobility data for longer‑term transport planning?

feasibility.rationale: The event is technically doable and similar one‑day car‑free initiatives have been run elsewhere, but the proposal lacks a concrete institutional lead, detailed budget, procurement and privacy safeguards. The consensus among experts leans toward moderate feasibility rather than high confidence.

novelty.rationale: Walking/biking days and gamified tracking apps exist globally; the combination of a city‑wide prize‑incentivised walk day with data sharing is a modest extension, giving the proposal a moderate level of originality.

impact.rationale: Quantitative emission reductions are modest (≈0.4 % of transport sector emissions) and rely on optimistic participation assumptions. However, the potential for awareness‑raising, health benefits, and generation of mobility data adds a moderate overall impact.

presentation.rationale: The document contains many typographical errors, inconsistent formatting, and fragmented sections, making it difficult to extract key information despite the inclusion of some quantitative estimates.

final.synthesis: All six experts agree that LET'S WALK is a feasible pilot if a clear governance structure is established. The main converging themes are: (1) the need for a designated lead agency or inter‑ministerial task force, a legal decree for temporary road reallocation, and a transparent financing plan; (2) the importance of equity measures such as non‑digital registration, accessible routes, and inclusive prize allocation to avoid marginalising low‑income, disabled, or informal‑sector workers; (3) the requirement for robust impact measurement, including pilot studies, sensitivity analysis of participation rates, and post‑event surveys to assess behavioural persistence and rebound effects; and (4) safety provisions through temporary pedestrian zones, reversible lane closures, and traffic‑calming measures. By integrating these complementary insights, the final judgment reflects a moderate feasibility, novelty, and impact rating, while highlighting the critical gaps that must be addressed before implementation.
final.uncertainty: The greatest uncertainties lie in (a) actual participation rates and the reliability of the tracking app’s emissions calculations; (b) securing a detailed budget and legal authority for road reallocation and data sharing; and (c) ensuring equitable access for populations without smartphones or the ability to work from home. These open questions could shift feasibility and impact scores up or down depending on how they are resolved.
